next_head_excerpt:
# Chapter 12

Tommy was alone on the deck for the first time since Saturday morning. Monday afternoon, the sun low enough to turn the lake copper, and he had maybe fifteen minutes before someone came out to check on him or ask if he wanted another round of Mallick's questions or share their feelings about Martin or whatever the fuck else these guys couldn't stop doing.

He'd pulled one of the Adirondack chairs to the rail where he could put his feet up and watch the water. His hands were around a can of Busch Light that had gone warm twenty minutes ago. He wasn't drinking it. He was holding it the way he held a football before a snap drill — not for the thing itself but because his hands needed something to do.

It had been four days. Four days since Martin, and the house felt like a locker room after a loss that shouldn't have happened — everybody walking around like they'd forgotten how hallways worked, picking up cups and putting them back down, starting sentences and letting them die. Paul had been in Martin's study all morning doing whatever Paul did when he was processing, which meant reorganizing information into categories nobody asked for. Joel was somewhere being Joel, which meant radiating controlled fury at a man who couldn't receive it anymore. Doug was on the couch reading the same page of a magazine he'd been reading since breakfast. Nate was in the basement doing something with his phone that Tommy hoped wasn't podcast-related but probably was.

And Mallick had gone back to Detroit Lakes. Working the case, supposedly. Tommy didn't believe a goddamn word of i...
open_hooks_to_carry: Paul in Martin's room going through his things — the intimacy and invasiveness of handling a dead friend's belongings
Paul finds Martin's will in the study: Martin left Tommy the lake house, not Paul
Memories of the Martin-Tommy dynamic across twenty-five years: protectiveness, tolerance, the way Martin always ensured Tommy came back
Paul begins to suspect a deeper bond — something that created obligation, not just friendship
The comedy in Paul's indignation should be real: he's also hurt that his best friend left the house to someone else
prev_tail_excerpt:
...lake house. The cases that weren't what they looked like were the ones where "probably" had a splinter in it — a single detail doing more work than it should, a piece of evidence that was clean when everything around it was dirty, or dirty when everything around it was clean.

She couldn't name the splinter. She couldn't even point to which piece of evidence contained it. But the shape of the case — five panics arranged around one natural death — had a lopsidedness she couldn't account for. Five explanations, each one sufficient, each one human, and still the picture tilted.

Maybe the tilt was nothing. Maybe this was just a dead man and five frightened friends and a casserole nobody ate and a county detective who'd been at this long enough to see ghosts in clean rooms.

She closed the notebook. She'd sleep on it. In the morning the shape would either settle flat or it wouldn't.

The refrigerator cycled off. The glasses in the cabinet above the sink stopped their rattling. Mallick rinsed her coffee mug, dried it, and set it on the counter next to the coffeemaker because she'd want it first thing and it was already clean and why open the cabinet twice. She put the foil back on the casserole dish, though she'd already put the foil back, so she was just pressing it down — a thing to do with her hands while her mind ran the grid one more time.

She turned off the kitchen light and went upstairs to the guest bedroom the deputies had cleared for her. The third step from the top creaked under her weight, and she wondered how many of the five had remembered to skip it in the dark.
secondary_character_beats: Paul's memories of Martin should include at least one that is purely funny and purely kind — the reader needs to grieve Martin too

setups_to_plant.description: Martin left Tommy the lake house in his will. This fact, combined with the fire document, will be the emotional trigger in Paul's confrontation with Tommy in Ch 17.

state_deltas_required: Paul's discovery that Martin's central loyalty was not to him but to Tommy
Paul's grief shifts from loss to suspicion — Martin was hiding something about Tommy, and the will is proof it wasn't casual
